does: This paper diagnoses and fixes a critical failure mode in learning-based long-range fringe projection profilometry (FPP), where depth is inferred from single images of projected fringe patterns. The authors identify that standard UNet baselines achieve only 14.54 mm mean absolute error on their synthetic benchmark by exploiting object-boundary shape priors rather than actually decoding fringe phase information. Using mechanistic interpretability probes (linear probing, Grad-CAM, flat-plane out-of-distribution tests) and conformal uncertainty quantification, they converge on a single failure locus: the network learns spurious shortcuts that ignore the underlying physics. They propose PhiCalNet, which repairs this by outputting wrapped phase directly instead of depth, forcing the architecture to solve the ill-posed single-shot problem through proper phase unwrapping. The repair is verified through the same diagnostic tools, demonstrating architectural alignment with physical measurement principles.
connects: This work builds on two converging research threads. First, it extends learning-based single-shot FPP, which has been predominantly studied in close-range regimes, into the challenging long-range domain (standoff beyond 1 m) where inverse-square intensity falloff and absent fringe-order information create inherent ill-posedness. Second, it applies mechanistic interpretability and uncertainty quantification as diagnostic tools—approaches gaining traction in ML safety and interpretability—to address domain-specific physics learning problems. The paper responds to limitations in prior FPP architectures that lack mechanistic understanding of what features they actually exploit, and it addresses the broader challenge of ensuring deep learning systems respect physical constraints and domain knowledge when learning inverse problems.
enables: This work enables more robust learning-based 3D reconstruction systems that respect physical principles rather than exploiting spurious correlations, particularly important for safety-critical applications like autonomous navigation and robotic manipulation in outdoor long-range scenarios. By demonstrating that mechanistic interpretability and conformal UQ can jointly diagnose and guide repair of architectural shortcuts, it provides a reproducible methodology for validating physics-informed machine learning across other inverse problems—metrology, imaging, geophysics. The approach suggests how to design architectures that are inherently more interpretable by aligning intermediate representations with domain structure (wrapped phase as an intermediate output). Future work could extend this diagnosis-repair framework to real captured data at long range, explore fully differentiable phase-unwrapping layers, and apply similar mechanistic verification strategies to other ill-posed measurement problems where deep learning might otherwise learn unphysical solutions.
